class_schedule.topic: The Art and Science of Diplomacy
learning_objectives: Define diplomacy and distinguish it from foreign policy
Trace the evolution of diplomatic practice from ancient to modern forms
Identify the functions of diplomacy: representation, negotiation, information, protection
readings: Berridge, Diplomacy: Theory and Practice Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Diplomacy is the primary instrument through which states manage their relations. It is both an ancient art and a modern profession.
key_concepts: Diplomacy vs. foreign policy: means vs. goals
Functions: representation, negotiation, reporting, protecting nationals
Evolution: Italian city-states, Congress of Vienna, modern multilateralism
The Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations (1961)
Diplomatic immunity and its rationale
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study: the role of shuttle diplomacy in the Camp David Accords (1978)

review.value: Representing the sending state, negotiating on its behalf, reporting conditions in the host state, and protecting nationals abroad
The treaty codifying diplomatic privileges and immunities, including inviolability of diplomatic premises and personnel

class_schedule.topic: Foreign Policy Analysis: Actors and Processes
learning_objectives: Identify the key actors in foreign policy making: executives, bureaucracies, legislatures, publics
Apply rational actor, organizational process, and bureaucratic politics models
Analyze how domestic politics constrain foreign policy
readings: Allison & Zelikow, Essence of Decision Ch. 1-3 (excerpts)
Putnam, 'Diplomacy and Domestic Politics: The Logic of Two-Level Games'

lecture_notes.introduction: Foreign policy does not emerge from a single rational mind. It is the product of competing bureaucracies, political pressures, and cognitive limitations.
key_concepts: Rational Actor Model (Model I): unitary state maximizing national interest
Organizational Process Model (Model II): standard operating procedures shape outcomes
Bureaucratic Politics Model (Model III): policy as outcome of bargaining
Putnam's two-level games: simultaneous domestic and international negotiation
Groupthink and cognitive biases in foreign policy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Applying all three Allison models to the Cuban Missile Crisis decision-making

review.value: Foreign policy outcomes result from bargaining among government officials with different interests, where 'where you stand depends on where you sit'
Leaders simultaneously negotiate at the international table (Level I) and seek domestic ratification (Level II), with the domestic 'win-set' constraining international agreements

learning_objectives: Distinguish between distributive and integrative negotiation
Apply principled negotiation (Fisher and Ury) to international disputes
Analyze the role of BATNA, ZOPA, and framing in diplomatic negotiations
readings: Fisher & Ury, Getting to Yes Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Negotiation is the core skill of diplomacy. Understanding its principles transforms how you approach any international dispute.
key_concepts: Distributive negotiation: fixed-pie, zero-sum bargaining
Integrative negotiation: expanding the pie through creative solutions
BATNA: Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement
ZOPA: Zone of Possible Agreement
Fisher and Ury's principled negotiation: separate people from problems, focus on interests not positions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Bilateral negotiation simulation: resolving a border river water-sharing dispute
setup_requirements: Negotiation simulation roles and confidential instructions

review.value: Your Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement determines your bargaining power: the better your BATNA, the more you can demand at the table
Separate people from problems, focus on interests not positions, invent options for mutual gain, insist on objective criteria

learning_objectives: Analyze the dynamics of multilateral negotiation in international organizations
Evaluate the role of summits and personal diplomacy between leaders
Assess the impact of coalition-building and bloc politics

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern diplomacy increasingly occurs in multilateral settings where dozens or hundreds of parties negotiate simultaneously. This creates unique dynamics.
key_concepts: Multilateral conference diplomacy: UN General Assembly, G7/G20, COP
Coalition-building and bloc politics (G77, EU common positions)
Summit diplomacy: advantages and risks of leader-to-leader engagement
Track II diplomacy: unofficial channels and back-channels
The role of mediators and good offices
lecture_notes.demonstration: Multilateral simulation: COP climate negotiation with developed, developing, and small island state delegations

review.value: Direct meetings between heads of state that can break deadlocks and build personal trust, but risk improvised commitments and domestic backlash
Unofficial dialogue between non-government experts and influential citizens that can explore options and build trust when official channels are blocked

class_schedule.topic: Economic Statecraft: Sanctions, Aid, and Coercive Diplomacy
learning_objectives: Analyze the use of economic sanctions as a foreign policy tool
Evaluate the effectiveness of sanctions based on empirical evidence
Distinguish between positive inducements and negative pressure in statecraft
readings: Hufbauer et al., Economic Sanctions Reconsidered Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Between diplomacy and war lies economic statecraft. Sanctions have become the tool of choice for states seeking to change behavior without military force.
key_concepts: Types of sanctions: comprehensive, targeted (smart), sectoral, secondary
Sanction objectives: coercion, constraining, signaling
Effectiveness debate: when do sanctions work?
Humanitarian consequences and sanctions design
Foreign aid as diplomatic leverage
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis: evaluating the effectiveness of sanctions on Iran's nuclear program

review.value: Targeted measures (asset freezes, travel bans, arms embargoes) aimed at decision-makers rather than entire populations, designed to reduce humanitarian harm
Research suggests sanctions succeed about one-third of the time, with higher success when demands are modest, the target is economically vulnerable, and multilateral coordination is strong

class_schedule.topic: 21st-Century Diplomacy: Digital, Public, and Crisis Diplomacy
learning_objectives: Analyze how digital technology has transformed diplomatic practice
Evaluate the role of public diplomacy and strategic communication
Apply crisis diplomacy principles to a simulated international crisis
readings: Hocking et al., Futures for Diplomacy Ch. 5-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Diplomacy in the 21st century operates in a transformed environment: social media, 24-hour news, cyber operations, and empowered publics all reshape the diplomatic landscape.
key_concepts: Digital diplomacy: social media, real-time communication, data analytics
Public diplomacy: communicating with foreign publics, not just governments
Crisis diplomacy: managing escalation under time pressure and uncertainty
Diplomatic signaling in the nuclear age
The democratization of foreign policy information
lecture_notes.demonstration: Crisis simulation: managing an escalating international incident through diplomatic channels in real time

review.value: Government-sponsored programs to inform, engage, and influence foreign publics, complementing traditional state-to-state diplomacy
The management of acute international crises through rapid communication, de-escalation signaling, face-saving compromises, and controlled pressure

questions.question: Compare the Rational Actor Model (Model I) with the Bureaucratic Politics Model (Model III) in explaining foreign policy decisions. Why might the same decision look different through each lens?
questions.answer: The Rational Actor Model treats the state as a unitary decision-maker that calculates costs and benefits to maximize national interest. The Bureaucratic Politics Model sees foreign policy as the outcome of bargaining among competing officials and agencies, each with different interests and perspectives. The same decision looks different because Model I explains what the state chose and why it was rational, while Model III reveals that the outcome was shaped by who had access to the president, which agency controlled implementation, and what compromises were struck. For example, a military intervention might appear as a rational deterrence move (Model I) but was actually driven by the Pentagon's institutional preference for action over the State Department's preference for negotiation (Model III).

questions.question: What is BATNA and why is it the most important concept in negotiation? How does a weak BATNA affect a diplomat's negotiating position?
questions.answer: BATNA is your Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement -- what you will do if negotiations fail. It is the most important concept because it defines your walkaway point: you should never accept an agreement worse than your BATNA. A strong BATNA gives you leverage because you can credibly threaten to leave the table. A weak BATNA (no good alternative) forces a diplomat to accept unfavorable terms because the cost of no agreement is even worse. For example, a small country with no alternative trade partners has a weak BATNA in bilateral trade negotiations and may have to accept lopsided terms.

rubric.key: Explains its importance for negotiation power
Describes the impact of a weak BATNA

questions.question: What are 'smart sanctions' and why were they developed as an alternative to comprehensive sanctions? Identify one advantage and one limitation.
questions.answer: Smart (targeted) sanctions are measures aimed specifically at decision-makers and elites responsible for objectionable policies, rather than at entire national populations. They include asset freezes, travel bans, arms embargoes, and targeted financial restrictions. They were developed because comprehensive sanctions (like those on Iraq in the 1990s) caused massive humanitarian suffering among ordinary citizens while regime leaders were insulated. Advantage: they reduce civilian harm while pressuring the responsible individuals directly. Limitation: elites often find ways to evade them through shell companies, intermediaries, and sympathetic states, reducing their coercive effect.

rubric.key: Explains the rationale for their development

questions.question: Explain Putnam's concept of two-level games. How can a narrow domestic 'win-set' actually strengthen a diplomat's hand in international negotiations?
questions.answer: Putnam's two-level games model says leaders simultaneously negotiate at the international table (Level I) and must secure domestic ratification (Level II). The 'win-set' is the range of international agreements that would be ratified domestically. Paradoxically, a narrow win-set (strong domestic constraints) can strengthen a diplomat's position because they can credibly say 'I would accept your proposal but my legislature/public won't ratify it,' forcing the other side to make concessions. For example, a US trade negotiator can cite Congressional opposition to push foreign partners for better terms, using domestic constraints as leverage rather than weakness.

rubric.key: Explains the paradox of narrow win-sets

major_assignments.title: Diplomatic Negotiation Simulation and Analysis
major_assignments.description: Participate in a multi-round diplomatic negotiation simulation and produce a comprehensive post-negotiation analysis evaluating strategy, process, and outcomes through the lens of negotiation theory and foreign policy analysis.
objectives: Apply negotiation theory in a simulated diplomatic context
Analyze decision-making through foreign policy models
Evaluate outcomes using principled negotiation criteria
Reflect critically on personal negotiation performance

criteria: Application of negotiation theory to simulation experience
Use of foreign policy models to explain dynamics
Honest assessment of what worked and what did not
Clear pre-negotiation strategy with priorities identified
Adaptation to changing dynamics during negotiation
Understanding of counterpart interests and constraints
Creative use of leverage and concessions

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on diplomatic history, theory, and case studies

simulation_participation.description: Performance in negotiation simulations and crisis exercises

major_assignments.description: Diplomatic negotiation analysis paper and presentation

midterm_examination.description: Exam on diplomatic history, foreign policy models, and negotiation theory

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam including a simulated diplomatic problem

course_policies.attendance: Attendance at simulation sessions is mandatory. Missing a simulation results in zero for that exercise with no make-up.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late papers penalized 5% per day. Strategy memos cannot be submitted late.
course_policies.collaboration: Simulation teams collaborate during exercises. All written analysis is individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All written work must be original. Proper citation of diplomatic sources and academic literature required.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to foreign policy databases, diplomatic archives, and course simulation platform.
